title: Monetary Theory, Economic Crises & the Instruments of Power

required.title: The General Theory of Employment, Interest and Money
A Monetary History of the United States, 1867-1960

supplementary.title: Crashed: How a Decade of Financial Crises Changed the World
Principles for Navigating Big Debt Crises

competence_markers.description: Can explain and apply MV = PQ, including its assumptions and limitations
Can describe the Cantillon effect and analyze its distributional consequences with real-world examples
Can narrate the Bretton Woods system, its collapse, and the transition to fiat money with historical specificity
Can explain the petrodollar system and its geopolitical implications
Can analyze at least two sovereign debt crises, identifying causes, mechanics, and lessons
Can trace the complete causal chain of the 2008 financial crisis from subprime lending through global recession
Can evaluate cryptocurrency as a monetary innovation, applying classical monetary criteria
Can present both the MMT argument and the orthodox critique fairly and identify the key assumptions in each
Can explain how sanctions, SWIFT exclusion, and reserve currency status function as instruments of power
Can read a central bank policy statement and analyze the tradeoffs involved in the decisions described

narration_prompts.prompt: Explain the quantity theory of money (MV = PQ) to someone who has completed Weathering Storms but has not encountered formal monetary theory. Make the equation intuitive, not just mathematical.
Describe the Cantillon effect and explain why it matters for monetary justice. Use a real-world example.
Tell the story of Bretton Woods — why it was created, how it worked, why it collapsed, and what replaced it.
Explain the 2008 financial crisis as a causal chain: what caused what, in sequence, from subprime lending through global recession.
A friend says cryptocurrency will replace government money. Another says it is a worthless speculation. Evaluate both claims with intellectual honesty.
Explain how sanctions and SWIFT exclusion function as instruments of power. What are their strengths and limitations?
Present the strongest version of the MMT argument, then present the strongest version of the orthodox critique. Where does the evidence point?

cross_connections.description: Every concept in this packet — inflation, monetary expansion, asset bubbles, the Cantillon effect — directly affects personal financial strategy. Understanding macro is necessary for navigating personal finance in a fiat money world.
MV = PQ, debt-to-GDP ratios, inflation calculations, and bubble analysis all require statistical and mathematical competence.
The petrodollar system, trade routes, resource distribution, and sanctions effectiveness are all geographically grounded.

Central banks, the IMF, the World Bank, SWIFT, and credit ratings agencies wield enormous power without direct democratic accountability. This packet provides the economic substance; 1.4.7 provides the power analysis framework.
The rise and fall of monetary orders (Roman debasement, Spanish silver inflation, British pound hegemony, Bretton Woods) are inseparable from civilizational trajectories.
The Federal Reserve's authority, congressional spending power, and the constitutionality of monetary policy raise fundamental questions about governance.
Sanctions, reserve currency privilege, and the petrodollar system are instruments of foreign policy. Economic power and military power are intertwined.
Macro theory rests on micro foundations. The 2008 crisis was built on millions of individual mortgage decisions, lending decisions, and investment decisions.

content.why_this_matters:
You already understand how banks work, what inflation is, and why the boom-bust cycle repeats. Now you are ready for the full picture: the formal theories that explain monetary systems, the institutional architecture that governs global finance, and the ways economic tools serve as instruments of geopolitical power. This is not academic exercise. The monetary order shapes who prospers and who suffers, which nations rise and which decline, and how power flows through the global system without a single shot being fired.

The post-1971 world operates on fiat money — currency backed by nothing but government authority and collective trust. This arrangement has consequences that most citizens never examine. New money does not enter the economy evenly; those closest to the source benefit first, at the expense of those furthest away. The dollar's role as the world's reserve currency grants the United States extraordinary privilege and imposes extraordinary responsibilities. Sovereign debt, once a manageable fiscal tool, has become a structural feature of every major economy, with implications that extend decades into the future.

This packet will equip you with the analytical tools to understand these systems from the inside. You will study the quantity theory of money, the Cantillon effect, the Bretton Woods order and its collapse, the petrodollar system, sovereign debt crises, the full mechanics of the 2008 financial crisis, cryptocurrency as monetary experiment, the Modern Monetary Theory debate, and the use of economic tools — sanctions, SWIFT exclusion, reserve currency status — as instruments of power. By the end, you will be able to read a central bank policy statement, analyze a sovereign debt crisis, evaluate competing monetary theories, and recognize when economic tools are being wielded for geopolitical ends.
content.what_this_develops:
1. Command of formal monetary theory including the quantity theory of money (MV = PQ) and its limitations
2. Understanding of the Cantillon effect and its implications for monetary justice
3. Thorough knowledge of the Bretton Woods system, its collapse, and the post-1971 fiat monetary order
4. Grasp of the petrodollar system and its geopolitical significance
5. Ability to analyze sovereign debt crises through historical case studies
6. Detailed understanding of the 2008 financial crisis — causes, mechanics, and consequences
7. Informed assessment of cryptocurrency and central bank digital currencies as monetary innovations
8. Capacity to evaluate the Modern Monetary Theory debate with intellectual honesty
9. Recognition of economic sanctions, SWIFT weaponization, and reserve currency privilege as instruments of power
content.core_content:
### The Quantity Theory of Money: MV = PQ

The quantity theory of money provides the foundational equation of monetary economics: **MV = PQ**, where M is the money supply, V is the velocity of money (how often each dollar is spent per period), P is the price level, and Q is the quantity of goods and services produced.

The equation is an identity — it is true by definition, since the total amount spent (MV) must equal the total value of what is sold (PQ). The theoretical power comes from the assumptions layered on top. Classical monetarists, following Friedman, argued that V is relatively stable in the short run and Q is determined by real factors (labor, capital, technology), so changes in M translate primarily into changes in P. In plain language: increasing the money supply causes inflation.

This framework has real explanatory power. It correctly predicts that large monetary expansions produce inflation (Zimbabwe, Venezuela, Weimar Germany). But it also has limitations. Velocity is not always stable — during financial crises, people and institutions hoard cash, velocity plummets, and massive monetary expansion can occur without proportional inflation (as the United States demonstrated after 2008). Understanding both the power and the limits of MV = PQ is essential to serious monetary analysis.

**Transmission mechanisms** describe how changes in the money supply actually reach the real economy. When the Fed buys bonds, it credits bank reserves. Banks can then lend more. Lower interest rates encourage borrowing for homes, businesses, and consumption. Money flows into the economy through credit channels. But these channels can be blocked — if banks are afraid to lend or borrowers are afraid to borrow, monetary expansion stalls. This is the **liquidity trap**, the condition Keynes described where monetary policy loses its effectiveness.

**The zero lower bound** refers to the practical floor on interest rates. The Fed cannot push rates significantly below zero because depositors would simply withdraw physical cash. When rates hit zero and the economy still needs stimulus, the Fed turns to **quantitative easing (QE)** — purchasing longer-term assets (mortgage-backed securities, longer-maturity Treasury bonds) to push down long-term interest rates and force money into riskier assets. QE was deployed on an enormous scale after 2008 and again during the COVID-19 pandemic. Whether it produces real economic improvement or merely inflates asset prices remains genuinely debated.

### The Cantillon Effect

Richard Cantillon, writing in the 1730s, observed something that modern economists often neglect: new money does not enter the economy evenly. When a central bank creates money, it enters through specific channels — typically through banks and financial institutions. Those closest to the source of new money receive it first, before prices have adjusted upward. They can buy assets at pre-inflation prices. By the time the new money percolates through the economy to wages and consumer prices, inflation has already eroded its value. Those furthest from the source — typically wage earners, savers, and the poor — receive the new money last, after prices have already risen.

The implication is profound: monetary expansion functions as a regressive transfer. It redistributes purchasing power from those furthest from the money spigot to those nearest. Asset owners (stocks, real estate, bonds) benefit as their holdings appreciate. Wage earners and cash savers lose as their purchasing power declines. This is not a conspiracy theory — it is a mechanical consequence of how money enters the economy. Understanding the Cantillon effect transforms how you evaluate monetary policy: every decision to expand the money supply has distributional consequences, and those consequences are not neutral.

### Bretton Woods and the Post-1971 Monetary Order

In July 1944, delegates from 44 Allied nations met at Bretton Woods, New Hampshire, to design the post-war monetary system. The system they created fixed exchange rates, with all currencies pegged to the U.S. dollar and the dollar pegged to gold at $35 per ounce. The dollar became the world's reserve currency — the standard in which international trade was denominated and in which nations held their reserves.

The system worked as long as the United States maintained discipline — as long as there were enough gold reserves to back the dollars in circulation. But through the 1960s, American spending on the Vietnam War and domestic programs (the "guns and butter" debate) led to an outflow of dollars that far exceeded gold reserves. Foreign governments, particularly France under de Gaulle, began demanding gold for their dollars. On August 15, 1971, President Nixon suspended the dollar's convertibility to gold. The Bretton Woods system was over.

What replaced it was the fiat money system we live in today. Currencies float against each other based on market forces and central bank intervention. No major currency is backed by any physical commodity. The value of a dollar rests entirely on trust — trust in the U.S. government, trust in the Federal Reserve, trust in the productivity of the American economy. This arrangement has enabled unprecedented monetary flexibility (central banks can expand and contract the money supply at will) but has also removed the external discipline that gold convertibility provided.

### The Petrodollar System

After the collapse of Bretton Woods, the dollar's global dominance was reinforced through a new mechanism. In 1974, the United States and Saudi Arabia reached an agreement: Saudi Arabia would price its oil in dollars and invest its surplus revenues in U.S. Treasury bonds. In return, the United States would provide military protection. Other OPEC nations followed suit. Because every nation needs oil, every nation needs dollars to buy oil. This creates constant global demand for dollars, which allows the United States to borrow more cheaply than any other country and to run persistent trade deficits without the currency collapse that would discipline any other nation.

The petrodollar system is a pillar of American economic hegemony. It is also a source of geopolitical tension. Nations that have attempted to price oil in other currencies — Iraq under Saddam Hussein (euros), Libya under Gaddafi (gold dinar) — have found themselves in conflict with the United States, though the causal relationship is debated. China and Russia have made ongoing efforts to reduce their dependence on the dollar. The long-term stability of the petrodollar system is one of the most consequential open questions in global economics.

### Sovereign Debt: Borrowing Against the Future

Sovereign debt is government borrowing — the sale of bonds promising to repay with interest, funded by future tax revenue. All major economies carry sovereign debt. The question is not whether government borrowing occurs but whether it remains sustainable.

Debt becomes dangerous when interest payments consume an increasing share of government revenue, leaving less for everything else — infrastructure, defense, social programs, investment. The debt-to-GDP ratio provides a rough measure: Japan's exceeds 250%, Italy's is around 140%, the United States' has surpassed 120%. High ratios do not automatically cause crisis — Japan has sustained its debt for decades. But high ratios reduce flexibility, make borrowing more expensive, and create vulnerability to confidence shocks.

**Greece, 2010.** Greece entered the eurozone with hidden fiscal problems. When the 2008 crisis exposed the true size of its debts, bond markets lost confidence. Interest rates on Greek debt spiked, making repayment impossible without external assistance. The resulting bailout conditions — severe austerity imposed by the troika (EU, ECB, IMF) — produced a depression that shrank the Greek economy by 25%. Unemployment exceeded 27%. The crisis exposed a fundamental tension in the eurozone: shared currency but no shared fiscal policy.

**Argentina, 2001.** Argentina had pegged its currency to the dollar during the 1990s, which eliminated inflation but made Argentine exports uncompetitive. When the economy contracted, the government could not devalue its currency (the peg prevented it) or expand its money supply. Sovereign debt became unsustainable. In December 2001, Argentina defaulted on $93 billion in debt — at the time, the largest sovereign default in history. Banks froze deposits. The economy collapsed. Five presidents cycled through in two weeks.

### The 2008 Financial Crisis: A Complete Anatomy

The 2008 crisis was not a random shock. It was the predictable outcome of a causal chain that took years to develop.

**Step 1: Subprime lending.** Banks and mortgage companies made loans to borrowers with poor credit histories and insufficient income — "subprime" borrowers. These loans often featured adjustable rates that started low and reset higher. The assumption: housing prices would keep rising, allowing borrowers to refinance before rates adjusted.

**Step 2: Securitization.** Banks bundled thousands of these mortgages into mortgage-backed securities (MBS) and sold them to investors worldwide. This dispersed the risk — or so it seemed. In reality, it obscured the risk. Investors buying MBS had no way to evaluate the underlying loans.

**Step 3: Credit default swaps (CDS).** Financial institutions created insurance-like contracts (CDS) against the default of mortgage-backed securities. AIG sold enormous quantities of CDS without maintaining reserves to pay claims. The CDS market grew far larger than the mortgage market itself, creating a web of interconnected obligations.

**Step 4: Ratings agency failure.** Credit ratings agencies (Moody's, Standard & Poor's, Fitch) rated many mortgage-backed securities AAA — the highest safety rating — despite the underlying loans being high-risk. The agencies were paid by the banks issuing the securities. The conflict of interest was structural.

**Step 5: The unraveling.** When housing prices stopped rising and began to fall, subprime borrowers defaulted. Mortgage-backed securities lost value. CDS claims triggered. AIG could not pay. Banks holding MBS faced massive losses. Credit froze. The entire financial system threatened to seize.

**Step 6: Bailout and aftermath.** The U.S. government intervened with $700 billion in emergency funds (TARP). The Fed dropped interest rates to zero and began unprecedented quantitative easing. Major institutions were bailed out. Lehman Brothers, which was not bailed out, collapsed — the largest bankruptcy in American history. The resulting recession destroyed trillions in wealth, cost millions of jobs, and reshaped the political landscape for a generation.

**Moral hazard** — the principle that protecting people from consequences encourages risky behavior — is the central unresolved tension of 2008. Banks took enormous risks knowing they might be considered "too big to fail." When they were indeed rescued, the lesson was reinforced: privatize the gains, socialize the losses.

### Cryptocurrency and Digital Currencies

Bitcoin, launched in 2009 by the pseudonymous Satoshi Nakamoto, is a monetary experiment born from the 2008 crisis. Its core innovation is a **blockchain** — a distributed ledger maintained by a network of computers, requiring no trusted central authority. Transactions are verified through proof-of-work computation. The total supply is capped at 21 million coins, making Bitcoin deflationary by design — the opposite of fiat currencies, which can be expanded without limit.

Bitcoin raises fundamental questions: Can money exist without government backing? Can a decentralized network replace central banks? Can a deflationary currency function as a medium of exchange, or does it become primarily a speculative asset? After more than fifteen years, the answers remain contested. Bitcoin has proven durable and has achieved significant adoption, but its volatility, energy consumption, and limited transaction throughput remain serious limitations.

**Central bank digital currencies (CBDCs)** represent the institutional response. China's digital yuan, the European Central Bank's digital euro project, and similar initiatives aim to provide the efficiency of digital currency under central bank control. CBDCs raise their own concerns: total surveillance of transactions, the ability to impose negative interest rates on individual accounts, and the potential to exclude individuals from the financial system with the flip of a switch. The tension between efficiency and liberty is not abstract here — it is architectural.

### The MMT Debate

Modern Monetary Theory (MMT) argues that a sovereign government issuing its own fiat currency can never run out of money. It can always create more. Therefore, the constraint on government spending is not revenue or borrowing capacity but inflation: the government should spend until the economy reaches full employment, using taxation not to fund spending but to control inflation and redistribute resources.

The orthodox critique is substantial. If governments spend without limit, trusting themselves to withdraw money (through taxation) before inflation spirals, the political incentives are misaligned. Politicians face elections; raising taxes to control inflation is politically painful. Historical examples of governments that printed money to fund spending — Weimar Germany, Zimbabwe, Venezuela — ended in hyperinflation and economic devastation.

MMT proponents respond that those examples involved special circumstances (war reparations, institutional collapse, commodity dependence) not applicable to developed nations with sophisticated economies. The debate is unresolved. What matters for your development is not which side is "right" but that you can articulate both positions honestly, identify the assumptions underlying each, and evaluate the evidence rather than adopting a position based on tribal affiliation.

### Economic Imperialism: The Instruments of Power

Economic tools serve as instruments of geopolitical power, often more effectively than military force.

**Sanctions** restrict a target nation's ability to trade, access financial markets, or use the international banking system. The United States has imposed comprehensive sanctions on Iran, North Korea, Cuba, and others. Sanctions can cripple economies — Iran's GDP contracted sharply under maximum pressure sanctions — but they also harm civilian populations and often fail to change regime behavior. The ethical and strategic calculus is complex.

**SWIFT weaponization.** SWIFT (Society for Worldwide Interbank Financial Telecommunication) is the messaging system that facilitates international bank transfers. Exclusion from SWIFT effectively disconnects a nation from the global financial system. When Russia was partially excluded from SWIFT after its 2022 invasion of Ukraine, the message was clear: financial infrastructure is a weapon. But the use of that weapon has accelerated efforts by China, Russia, and others to build alternative systems, potentially fragmenting the global financial order.

**Reserve currency privilege.** The dollar's status as the world's primary reserve currency grants the United States what former French Finance Minister Valery Giscard d'Estaing called an "exorbitant privilege." The United States can borrow in its own currency at lower rates than any other nation, run persistent deficits, and fund military operations worldwide. This privilege is not permanent. The British pound was the world's reserve currency before the dollar. If confidence in the dollar erodes — through excessive debt, aggressive weaponization of the financial system, or the emergence of credible alternatives — the privilege could diminish, with enormous consequences for American fiscal capacity and global influence.
content.formative_practices:
1. **MV = PQ Application.** The Federal Reserve doubles the money supply over a five-year period. GDP (Q) grows 15% over the same period. Velocity (V) remains roughly constant. What happens to the price level (P)? Show your work. Then discuss: what if velocity had declined by 30% during the same period? How would that change your answer?

2. **Cantillon Analysis.** After the 2008 crisis, the Fed engaged in massive quantitative easing. Trace how the new money entered the economy. Who received it first? What assets appreciated? Who benefited most? Who was left behind? Use specific evidence.

3. **Bretton Woods Counterfactual.** What if Nixon had not suspended gold convertibility in 1971? What would the consequences have been? What tradeoffs would the United States have faced? Is there an argument that abandoning gold was the right decision despite its costs?

4. **Sovereign Debt Comparison.** Compare the Greek crisis (2010) and the Argentine crisis (2001). What did they have in common? How did they differ? What made each nation vulnerable? What lessons do they offer for countries currently carrying high debt loads?

5. **2008 Crisis Responsibility.** Assign responsibility for the 2008 crisis across the following actors: mortgage lenders, investment banks, ratings agencies, regulators, the Federal Reserve, and individual borrowers. Who bears the most blame? Who the least? Defend your analysis.

6. **Bitcoin Assessment.** Read Nakamoto's original whitepaper. Evaluate Bitcoin as money using the classical criteria: medium of exchange, unit of account, store of value. Where does it succeed? Where does it fail? What would need to change for it to function as a true currency rather than a speculative asset?

7. **Sanctions Ethics.** The United States imposes comprehensive sanctions on a hostile regime. The regime's behavior does not change. The civilian population suffers severe deprivation. Evaluate the ethical and strategic case for continuing, modifying, or lifting the sanctions. What frameworks help you think through this?

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### Formal Monetary Theory (MV = PQ)

**Developing well:** The student can explain the equation, identify its components, distinguish the identity from the causal theory, explain why V and Q matter as much as M, and apply the framework to real scenarios. They can explain why massive post-2008 QE did not produce proportional consumer inflation (velocity declined; money went into asset prices, not consumer spending). They understand the zero lower bound and can explain why it matters.

**Developing slowly:** The student can state the equation and define its variables but cannot apply it to real situations. They treat MV = PQ as a simple rule ("more money = more inflation") without engaging with the complications.

**Diagnostic questions:** "The Fed tripled the monetary base between 2008 and 2014. Consumer inflation remained around 2%. Does this disprove the quantity theory? Explain." "What is the zero lower bound, and why does it force central banks to use unconventional tools?"

### The Cantillon Effect

**Developing well:** The student can explain the mechanism (new money enters unevenly, benefiting those nearest the source first), identify real-world examples (QE inflating asset prices while wages stagnate), and articulate the distributional justice implications without falling into conspiracy thinking.

**Developing slowly:** The student either does not grasp the mechanism or grasps it but presents it as a conspiracy ("the Fed deliberately enriches the rich") rather than as a structural consequence of how money enters the economy.

**Diagnostic questions:** "When the Fed buys bonds from banks, where does the new money go first? How does it reach ordinary workers? Does it arrive with the same purchasing power?" "Is the Cantillon effect a deliberate policy choice or a structural feature of monetary expansion?"

### Bretton Woods and the Post-1971 Order

**Developing well:** The student can narrate the creation, operation, and collapse of Bretton Woods with historical specificity. They understand why the system was created (post-war stability), how it worked (dollar-gold peg, fixed exchange rates), why it collapsed (overextension of dollar supply, French gold demands, Vietnam War spending), and what replaced it (floating fiat currencies). They can evaluate tradeoffs: gold discipline versus monetary flexibility.

**Developing slowly:** The student knows that Bretton Woods existed and that Nixon ended gold convertibility, but cannot explain the mechanics of the system or the forces that led to its collapse.

**Diagnostic questions:** "Why did de Gaulle demand gold for France's dollar reserves? What was he responding to?" "What did the United States gain and lose by abandoning gold convertibility?"

### The 2008 Financial Crisis

**Developing well:** The student can trace the complete causal chain — subprime lending, securitization, CDSs, ratings agency failure, the unraveling, the bailout — with specificity. They understand moral hazard and can discuss the "too big to fail" problem. They can assign responsibility across multiple actors and defend their analysis.

**Developing slowly:** The student has a general sense that "banks made bad loans and the economy crashed" but cannot trace the specific mechanisms. They assign blame to a single actor (banks, the government, borrowers) without seeing the systemic nature of the failure.

**Diagnostic questions:** "How did a mortgage default in Florida affect a pension fund in Norway?" "What role did credit ratings agencies play, and why did they fail?" "What is moral hazard, and how did the bailout potentially reinforce it?"

### Cryptocurrency Assessment

**Developing well:** The student can explain blockchain mechanics at a conceptual level, evaluate Bitcoin against classical monetary criteria (medium of exchange, unit of account, store of value), identify both its innovations and its limitations, and discuss CBDCs with awareness of the surveillance and control implications.

**Developing slowly:** The student either uncritically advocates for cryptocurrency ("it will replace the dollar") or uncritically dismisses it ("it is a Ponzi scheme") without serious engagement with the evidence and arguments.

**Diagnostic questions:** "Bitcoin's supply is capped at 21 million. What are the advantages and disadvantages of a deflationary monetary system?" "A central bank digital currency could allow the government to track every transaction and program money to expire. What are the implications?"
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Ideological Capture

This is the most significant danger at Standing Ground. The student may encounter compelling advocates for Austrian economics, MMT, gold-standard restoration, Bitcoin maximalism, or progressive monetary policy. Each of these frameworks contains genuine insights and genuine blind spots. Your job is to prevent premature ideological capture — the student latching onto one framework and filtering all evidence through it. Require them to present the strongest counterargument to every position they hold. If they cannot do so honestly, they are not yet thinking clearly.

### Confusing the Identity with the Theory

MV = PQ is true by definition. The quantity theory of money is a set of assumptions about V and Q that turns the identity into a predictive framework. Students routinely confuse these. The identity tells you that total spending equals total output. The theory tells you that money supply growth causes inflation (under certain conditions). Make sure the student understands the distinction.

### Treating 2008 as a Morality Play

The 2008 crisis lends itself to moralistic narratives: greedy bankers, irresponsible borrowers, corrupt regulators. There is truth in all of these, but the student must move beyond moral outrage to systemic analysis. What structural incentives produced the behavior? Why did rational actors, each pursuing their own interests, collectively create a catastrophe? The systemic question is more important than the moral one.

### Conspiracy Thinking About the Monetary System

The Cantillon effect, the petrodollar system, and the Fed's operations are genuinely concerning features of the modern monetary order. They raise legitimate questions about power, accountability, and distributive justice. But they are also lightning rods for conspiracy theories — shadowy cabals controlling the world through money creation. Teach the student to distinguish between structural critique (the monetary system has features that produce unjust outcomes) and conspiracy theory (a secret group deliberately designed the system to oppress). The former is analytical; the latter is a failure of analysis.

### Superficial Engagement with Primary Sources

Assigning Keynes, Friedman, and Nakamoto is meaningless if the student skims the texts or relies on summaries. At this tier, primary source engagement must be genuine. Discuss specific passages. Ask the student what Keynes meant by "animal spirits" or why Friedman attributed the Great Depression to Federal Reserve policy rather than market failure. If the student cannot engage with the actual arguments in the texts, they have not done the reading.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: MV = PQ for a Weathering Storms Student

**Strong narration:** The student explains the equation clearly without assuming mathematical background, makes the concept intuitive (total spending must equal total value of goods sold), explains what each variable represents with real examples, and discusses what happens when M changes. They acknowledge complications (V can change, Q responds to policy) without getting lost in them.

**Weak narration:** The student recites the equation and defines variables but cannot make it intuitive. Or they oversimplify: "more money means higher prices, always."

### Prompt 2: The Cantillon Effect

**Strong narration:** The student explains the mechanism clearly, provides a specific real-world example (e.g., post-2008 QE inflating stock and real estate prices while wages remained flat), and articulates the justice implications — monetary expansion is not distributionally neutral. They frame this as a structural feature, not a conspiracy.

**Weak narration:** The student describes the effect abstractly without concrete examples, or frames it conspiratorially without analytical precision.

### Prompt 3: The Story of Bretton Woods

**Strong narration:** A coherent narrative covering the post-war context, the structure of the system, how it operated, the pressures that undermined it, Nixon's decision, and what replaced it. The student evaluates tradeoffs: what was gained and lost at each stage.

**Weak narration:** "After World War II, currencies were tied to the dollar and the dollar was tied to gold. Then Nixon ended it." Correct but devoid of analysis, causation, or evaluation.

### Prompt 4: 2008 Causal Chain

**Strong narration:** The student walks through each link in the chain — subprime lending, securitization, CDSs, ratings failure, the unraveling, the bailout — explaining how each step led to the next. They demonstrate understanding of why the crisis was systemic rather than isolated.

**Weak narration:** "Banks gave loans to people who couldn't pay, then the banks failed." Accurate at the highest level but missing the mechanics that made this a global crisis rather than a local banking problem.

### Prompt 5: Cryptocurrency Evaluation

**Strong narration:** The student engages seriously with both the bull case (decentralization, fixed supply, censorship resistance, trustless architecture) and the bear case (volatility, energy consumption, limited adoption, speculative dynamics). They apply classical monetary criteria and arrive at a nuanced assessment.

**Weak narration:** An enthusiastic or dismissive take that does not seriously engage with the opposing arguments.

### Prompt 6: Sanctions and SWIFT

**Strong narration:** The student explains the mechanics (how sanctions restrict trade and financial access, how SWIFT exclusion disconnects nations from the global system), evaluates effectiveness with specific examples, discusses civilian harm, and addresses the strategic question of whether weaponizing financial infrastructure accelerates the development of alternatives.

**Weak narration:** "Sanctions punish bad countries" or "sanctions are economic warfare against civilians" — one-dimensional takes without analytical depth.

### Prompt 7: The MMT Debate

**Strong narration:** The student presents the MMT position accurately and charitably (sovereign currency issuers cannot run out of money; the real constraint is inflation, not revenue; taxation serves inflation control and redistribution). They then present the orthodox critique with equal rigor (political incentives make timely taxation unlikely; historical examples of money-printing disasters; MMT's assumptions may not hold under stress). They identify the key empirical question: would developed nations' political systems actually implement the taxation needed to control inflation?

**Weak narration:** The student presents one side fairly and dismisses the other as obviously wrong, or presents both sides in caricature without genuine engagement.
